1000→
  1001→NewDefinition Fin.elim0
  1002→
  1003→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
  1004→import Game.Metadata
  1005→
  1006→World "MeetFin"
  1007→Level 15
  1008→
  1009→Title "The Unique Element"
  1010→
  1011→Introduction "
  1012→# Fin 1 Has Exactly One Element
  1013→
  1014→`Fin 1` is the type of natural numbers less than 1. The only such
  1015→number is 0, so `Fin 1` has **exactly one element**.
  1016→
  1017→This means every `x : Fin 1` must equal `0`. To prove this, we'll
  1018→introduce a new tactic: **`ext`**.
  1019→
  1020→The `ext` tactic is shorthand for `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` — it converts
  1021→a goal `a = b` (for `Fin` elements) into `a.val = b.val`. The name
  1022→stands for *extensionality*: two structured values are equal when
  1023→their components are equal.
  1024→
  1025→`ext` is a **general-purpose tactic** — it works not just for `Fin`,
  1026→but for any type with an `@[ext]` attribute (functions, sets, and
  1027→many other types you'll meet later). Learning it here on `Fin` will
  1028→pay off when you encounter function extensionality in later worlds.
  1029→
  1030→**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 1` equals `0`.
  1031→Use `ext` then `omega`.
  1032→"
  1033→
  1034→/-- `Fin 1` has exactly one element: `0`. -/
  1035→Statement (x : Fin 1) : x = 0 := by
  1036→  Hint "Use `ext` to convert the `Fin` equality to a value equality.
  1037→  This is the same as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` but shorter."
  1038→  ext
  1039→  Hint "Now the goal is `x.val = 0`. Since `x : Fin 1`, you know
  1040→  `x.val < 1`, so `x.val = 0`. `omega` handles this."
  1041→  omega
  1042→
  1043→Conclusion "
  1044→`Fin 1` is a **singleton type** — it has exactly one element, which
  1045→is `0`.
  1046→
  1047→The `ext` tactic did the same work as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]`: it reduced
  1048→`Fin` equality to value equality. From now on, you can use either —
  1049→`ext` is shorter, `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` is more explicit.
  1050→
  1051→This is the **Fin equality pattern** from Level 10: reduce to values,
  1052→then compute. Whether you write `ext; omega` or
  1053→`rw [Fin.ext_iff]; omega`, the effect is the same.
  1054→
  1055→| `Fin n` | Number of elements | Character |
  1056→|---|---|---|
  1057→| `Fin 0` | 0 | Empty |
  1058→| `Fin 1` | 1 | Singleton |
  1059→| `Fin 2` | 2 | Coming soon! |
  1060→"
  1061→
  1062→/-- `ext` proves equality of structured types by reducing to equality
  1063→of their components.
  1064→
  1065→## Syntax
  1066→```
  1067→ext
  1068→```
  1069→
  1070→## When to use it
  1071→When the goal is `a = b` where the type has an `@[ext]` attribute.
  1072→
  1073→For `Fin n`: converts `a = b` to `a.val = b.val`.
  1074→For functions: converts `f = g` to `∀ x, f x = g x` (called `funext`).
  1075→
  1076→## Example
  1077→```
  1078→-- Goal: x = 0  (where x : Fin 1)
  1079→ext
  1080→-- Goal: x.val = (0 : Fin 1).val
  1081→```
  1082→
  1083→`ext` is a general-purpose tactic that works for many types beyond
  1084→`Fin`. It replaces `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` for Fin equality goals.
  1085→-/
  1086→TacticDoc ext
  1087→
  1088→NewTactic ext
  1089→
  1090→DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
  1091→DisabledTheorem Unique.eq_default Subsingleton.elim
  1092→import Game.Metadata
  1093→
  1094→World "MeetFin"
  1095→Level 16
  1096→
  1097→Title "Destructuring Fin"
  1098→
  1099→Introduction "
  1100→# Destructuring Fin Elements
  1101→
  1102→So far, you've accessed the parts of a `Fin n` element using dot
  1103→notation: `x.val` and `x.isLt`. There's another way:
  1104→**destructuring** with `cases`.
  1105→
  1106→When you write:
  1107→```
  1108→cases x with | mk v hlt =>
  1109→```
  1110→Lean replaces `x : Fin 3` with two new hypotheses:
  1111→- `v : ℕ` — the underlying value
  1112→- `hlt : v < 3` — the bound proof
  1113→
  1114→This is the same information as `x.val` and `x.isLt`, but now they're
  1115→standalone variables in your context.
  1116→
  1117→Why learn a second way to access these? Because in the next level,
  1118→you'll need to **case-split on the value itself** — asking whether
  1119→`v = 0`, `v = 1`, or something else. That requires `v` as a standalone
  1120→variable, which destructuring provides.
  1121→
  1122→**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 3` can be reconstructed
  1123→from a natural number and a bound proof. In other words, show there
  1124→exist `v` and `h : v < 3` such that `x = ⟨v, h⟩`.
  1125→
  1126→Destructure `x` first — after that, the components are right there in
  1127→your context and you can package them up.
  1128→"
  1129→
  1130→/-- Every Fin element can be reconstructed from its components. -/
  1131→Statement (x : Fin 3) : ∃ (v : ℕ) (h : v < 3), x = ⟨v, h⟩ := by
  1132→  Hint "Destructure `x` into its components:
  1133→  `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
  1134→  cases x with
  1135→  | mk v hlt =>
  1136→    Hint "Now you have `v : ℕ` and `hlt : v < 3` as standalone
  1137→    variables. The goal asks for a number and a bound proof such that
  1138→    the element equals the reconstruction. You already have both!
  1139→    Use `exact ⟨v, hlt, rfl⟩` to provide `v` as the number, `hlt`
  1140→    as the bound proof, and `rfl` for the equality (since the element
  1141→    IS its own reconstruction)."
  1142→    exact ⟨v, hlt, rfl⟩
  1143→
  1144→Conclusion "
  1145→You've just destructured a `Fin` element for the first time.
  1146→
  1147→The pattern `cases x with | mk v hlt =>` is the `Fin` version of
  1148→a general Lean pattern: any structure (or subtype) can be taken
  1149→apart with `cases` to expose its components.
  1150→
  1151→| Before destructuring | After |
  1152→|---|---|
  1153→| `x : Fin 3` | `v : ℕ`, `hlt : v < 3` |
  1154→| `x.val` | `v` |
  1155→| `x.isLt` | `hlt` |
  1156→
  1157→After destructuring, the components are standalone variables that
  1158→you can use directly — as existential witnesses, in case splits, or
  1159→in any other tactic that needs a named term. The equality

  1161→own reconstruction from components.

  1164→Lean: `obtain ⟨v, hlt⟩ := x`. This does the same thing as

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 15

Title "The Unique Element"

Introduction "
# Fin 1 Has Exactly One Element

`Fin 1` is the type of natural numbers less than 1. The only such
number is 0, so `Fin 1` has **exactly one element**.

This means every `x : Fin 1` must equal `0`. To prove this, we'll
introduce a new tactic: **`ext`**.

The `ext` tactic is shorthand for `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` — it converts
a goal `a = b` (for `Fin` elements) into `a.val = b.val`. The name
stands for *extensionality*: two structured values are equal when
their components are equal.

`ext` is a **general-purpose tactic** — it works not just for `Fin`,
but for any type with an `@[ext]` attribute (functions, sets, and
many other types you'll meet later). Learning it here on `Fin` will
pay off when you encounter function extensionality in later worlds.

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 1` equals `0`.
Use `ext` then `omega`.
"

/-- `Fin 1` has exactly one element: `0`. -/
Statement (x : Fin 1) : x = 0 := by
  Hint "Use `ext` to convert the `Fin` equality to a value equality.
  This is the same as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` but shorter."
  ext
  Hint "Now the goal is `x.val = 0`. Since `x : Fin 1`, you know
  `x.val < 1`, so `x.val = 0`. `omega` handles this."
  omega

Conclusion "
`Fin 1` is a **singleton type** — it has exactly one element, which
is `0`.

The `ext` tactic did the same work as `rw [Fin.ext_iff]`: it reduced
`Fin` equality to value equality. From now on, you can use either —
`ext` is shorter, `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` is more explicit.

This is the **Fin equality pattern** from Level 10: reduce to values,
then compute. Whether you write `ext; omega` or
`rw [Fin.ext_iff]; omega`, the effect is the same.

| `Fin n` | Number of elements | Character |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin 0` | 0 | Empty |
| `Fin 1` | 1 | Singleton |
| `Fin 2` | 2 | Coming soon! |
"

/-- `ext` proves equality of structured types by reducing to equality
of their components.

## Syntax
```
ext
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `a = b` where the type has an `@[ext]` attribute.

For `Fin n`: converts `a = b` to `a.val = b.val`.
For functions: converts `f = g` to `∀ x, f x = g x` (called `funext`).

## Example
```
-- Goal: x = 0  (where x : Fin 1)
ext
-- Goal: x.val = (0 : Fin 1).val
```

`ext` is a general-purpose tactic that works for many types beyond
`Fin`. It replaces `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` for Fin equality goals.
-/
TacticDoc ext

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 16

Title "Destructuring Fin"

Introduction "
# Destructuring Fin Elements

So far, you've accessed the parts of a `Fin n` element using dot
notation: `x.val` and `x.isLt`. There's another way:
**destructuring** with `cases`.

When you write:
```
cases x with | mk v hlt =>
```
Lean replaces `x : Fin 3` with two new hypotheses:
- `v : ℕ` — the underlying value
- `hlt : v < 3` — the bound proof

This is the same information as `x.val` and `x.isLt`, but now they're
standalone variables in your context.

Why learn a second way to access these? Because in the next level,
you'll need to **case-split on the value itself** — asking whether
`v = 0`, `v = 1`, or something else. That requires `v` as a standalone
variable, which destructuring provides.

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 3` can be reconstructed
from a natural number and a bound proof. In other words, show there
exist `v` and `h : v < 3` such that `x = ⟨v, h⟩`.

Destructure `x` first — after that, the components are right there in
your context and you can package them up.
"

/-- Every Fin element can be reconstructed from its components. -/
Statement (x : Fin 3) : ∃ (v : ℕ) (h : v < 3), x = ⟨v, h⟩ := by
  Hint "Destructure `x` into its components:
  `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
  cases x with
  | mk v hlt =>
    Hint "Now you have `v : ℕ` and `hlt : v < 3` as standalone
    variables. The goal asks for a number and a bound proof such that
    the element equals the reconstruction. You already have both!
    Use `exact ⟨v, hlt, rfl⟩` to provide `v` as the number, `hlt`
    as the bound proof, and `rfl` for the equality (since the element
    IS its own reconstruction)."
    exact ⟨v, hlt, rfl⟩

Conclusion "
You've just destructured a `Fin` element for the first time.

The pattern `cases x with | mk v hlt =>` is the `Fin` version of
a general Lean pattern: any structure (or subtype) can be taken
apart with `cases` to expose its components.

| Before destructuring | After |
|---|---|
| `x : Fin 3` | `v : ℕ`, `hlt : v < 3` |
| `x.val` | `v` |
| `x.isLt` | `hlt` |

After destructuring, the components are standalone variables that
you can use directly — as existential witnesses, in case splits, or
in any other tactic that needs a named term. The equality
`⟨v, hlt⟩ = ⟨v, hlt⟩` closes by `rfl` because the element IS its
own reconstruction from components.

**Alternative: `obtain`**. There is another way to destructure in
Lean: `obtain ⟨v, hlt⟩ := x`. This does the same thing as
`cases x with | mk v hlt =>` when the type has exactly one
constructor (as `Fin` does — its only constructor is `mk`). Use
`cases` when you need to enumerate multiple constructors (like
`Nat.zero` and `Nat.succ`); use `obtain` when there's only one
constructor and you just want to extract the components. Both
are common in real Lean code.

You'll need destructuring in the next level for full case analysis.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 17

Title "Two Elements"

Introduction "
# Case Analysis on Fin 2

`Fin 2` has two elements: `0` and `1`. To prove something about an
arbitrary `x : Fin 2`, you can **case-split**: handle each possible
value of `x` separately.

In Level 16, you learned to destructure a `Fin` element with
`cases x with | mk v hlt =>`. Now you'll take the next step:
**case-splitting on the natural number** to handle each value.

After destructuring, `v : ℕ` and `hlt : v < 2`. Use
`cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...` to split:
- `v = 0`: the element is `0`, close with `left; rfl`
- `v = n + 1`: split again on `n`
  - `n = 0`: the element is `1`, close with `right; rfl`
  - `n = succ m`: now `v ≥ 2`, contradicting `hlt : v < 2`

content.thinking: Let me continue reading the remaining MeetFin levels, and then move on to the other worlds.

content.content:
1200→
  1201→For the impossible case, you'll use a new tactic: `absurd`.
  1202→`absurd h1 h2` closes a goal when `h1 : P` and `h2 : ¬P`. Here,
  1203→`hlt` says `v < 2` but `v ≥ 2`, so `exact absurd hlt (by omega)` works.
  1204→
  1205→**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 2` is either `0` or `1`.
  1206→"
  1207→
  1208→/-- Every element of `Fin 2` is either `0` or `1`. -/
  1209→Statement (x : Fin 2) : x = 0 ∨ x = 1 := by
  1210→  Hint "Start by destructuring `x` into its components:
  1211→  `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
  1212→  cases x with
  1213→  | mk v hlt =>
  1214→    Hint "Now `v : ℕ` and `hlt : v < 2`. Case-split on `v`:
  1215→    `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
  1216→    cases v with
  1217→    | zero =>
  1218→      Hint "In this case `v = 0`, so `x` is the element `0`.
  1219→      Choose the left disjunct with `left`, then close with `rfl`."
  1220→      left
  1221→      rfl
  1222→    | succ n =>
  1223→      Hint "Now `v = n + 1`. Case-split once more on `n` to determine
  1224→      whether `v = 1` or `v ≥ 2`."
  1225→      cases n with
  1226→      | zero =>
  1227→        Hint "Now `v = 1`. Choose the right disjunct with `right`,
  1228→        then close with `rfl`."
  1229→        right
  1230→        rfl
  1231→      | succ m =>
  1232→        Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = m + 2`, but `hlt` says `v < 2`.
  1233→        This is impossible — `exact absurd hlt (by omega)` closes it."
  1234→        exact absurd hlt (by omega)
  1235→
  1236→Conclusion "
  1237→You've just performed **exhaustive case analysis** on `Fin 2`.
  1238→
  1239→The strategy — which we'll call **Fin case analysis** — is:
  1240→1. **Destructure** the `Fin` element (Level 16)
  1241→2. **Case-split** on the natural number (nested `cases` as needed)
  1242→3. **Handle** each case: valid cases with `left`/`right` + `rfl`,
  1243→   impossible cases with `absurd` + `omega`
  1244→
  1245→**Why does this work?** Exhaustive case analysis on `Fin n` terminates
  1246→because `.isLt` bounds the natural number. Each `cases` step peels
  1247→off one possible value (0, then 1, then 2, ...), and the `.isLt`
  1248→bound ensures that after `n` valid cases, the remaining case is
  1249→impossible — the value would need to be ≥ `n`, contradicting `v < n`.
  1250→This is the **Fin contradiction pattern** from Level 13 applied to the
  1251→impossible branch. This termination is what makes `Fin n` a *finite*
  1252→type in a proof-relevant sense: you can enumerate all elements and
  1253→handle each one. This idea will be formalized as `Fintype` in a later
  1254→world.
  1255→
  1256→This strategy works for any `Fin n` by nesting enough `cases` on the
  1257→natural number. But imagine doing this for `Fin 100`: you'd need 100
  1258→branches, 99 of which are contradiction proofs. The `fin_cases` tactic
  1259→(currently disabled) automates all of this — it performs the case split
  1260→and handles the impossible branches automatically. You'll unlock it
  1261→later.
  1262→
  1263→Fun fact: `Fin 2` is equivalent to `Bool` — both have exactly two
  1264→elements. Where `Bool` has `true` and `false`, `Fin 2` has `0` and `1`.
  1265→
  1266→**A note on `absurd`**: You used `absurd` here for a *bound violation*
  1267→in case analysis — the value was too large for the bound. This is a
  1268→general tool you'll use in later worlds whenever you have contradictory
  1269→hypotheses. In the next level, you'll practice case analysis on `Fin 3`,
  1270→and after that you'll learn a different pattern (`intro h; cases h`)
  1271→for when *constructors themselves* don't match (like `0 = 1`).
  1272→"
  1273→
  1274→/-- `absurd` closes a goal when you have contradictory hypotheses.
  1275→
  1276→## Syntax
  1277→```
  1278→exact absurd h1 h2
  1279→```
  1280→where `h1 : P` and `h2 : ¬P` (or `h1 : ¬P` and `h2 : P`).
  1281→
  1282→## When to use it
  1283→When you have a proposition and its negation in your hypotheses,
  1284→or when you can derive one of them from context.
  1285→
  1286→## Example
  1287→```
  1288→-- hlt : v < 2
  1289→-- But v = m + 2, so v < 2 is impossible
  1290→exact absurd hlt (by omega)
  1291→```
  1292→
  1293→## Note
  1294→`absurd` is the formal version of proof by contradiction:
  1295→if you can show both `P` and `¬P`, the current goal is closed.
  1296→-/
  1297→TacticDoc absurd
  1298→

  1305→World "MeetFin"
  1306→Level 18
  1307→
  1308→Title "Case Analysis Practice"
  1309→
  1310→Introduction "
  1311→# Practice: Case Analysis on Fin 3
  1312→
  1313→In Level 17, you exhausted all cases of `Fin 2` (two valid, one
  1314→impossible). Now try `Fin 3` — the same strategy with one more valid
  1315→case.
  1316→
  1317→**Why are some tactics disabled?** You might wonder why tactics like
  1318→`fin_cases`, `decide`, and `norm_num` are unavailable. These tactics
  1319→can automate case analysis and arithmetic in one step — which is
  1320→exactly why they're disabled here. The manual approach forces you to
  1321→understand *what* the automation does: destructure the `Fin` element,
  1322→split on the natural number, handle each valid case, and dismiss
  1323→impossible cases with `absurd`. Once you understand this structure,
  1324→you'll appreciate the automation when it's unlocked later. The
  1325→automation doesn't replace this understanding — it just lets you
  1326→skip the mechanical parts once you've mastered them.
  1327→
  1328→The pattern is the same:
  1329→1. **Destructure**: `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`
  1330→2. **Case-split on `v`**: `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`
  1331→3. **Nest further** as needed
  1332→4. **Valid cases**: `left`/`right` + `rfl`
  1333→5. **Impossible cases**: `exact absurd hlt (by omega)`
  1334→
  1335→For `Fin 3`, you'll have three valid cases (`v = 0, 1, 2`) and one
  1336→impossible case (`v ≥ 3`).
  1337→
  1338→**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 3` is `0`, `1`, or `2`.
  1339→"
  1340→
  1341→/-- Every element of `Fin 3` is `0`, `1`, or `2`. -/
  1342→Statement (x : Fin 3) : x = 0 ∨ x = 1 ∨ x = 2 := by
  1343→  Hint "Start by destructuring: `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
  1344→  cases x with
  1345→  | mk v hlt =>
  1346→    Hint "Case-split on `v`: `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
  1347→    cases v with
  1348→    | zero =>
  1349→      Hint "Here `v = 0`. Choose the left disjunct: `left` then `rfl`."
  1350→      left
  1351→      rfl
  1352→    | succ n =>
  1353→      Hint "Now `v = n + 1`. Case-split again on `n`."
  1354→      cases n with
  1355→      | zero =>
  1356→        Hint "Here `v = 1`. Choose `right` then `left` then `rfl`."
  1357→        right
  1358→        left
  1359→        rfl
  1360→      | succ m =>
  1361→        Hint "Now `v = m + 2`. One more case split on `m`."
  1362→        cases m with
  1363→        | zero =>
  1364→          Hint "Here `v = 2`. Choose `right` then `right` then `rfl`."
  1365→          right
  1366→          right
  1367→          rfl
  1368→        | succ k =>
  1369→          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hlt` says `v < 3`.
  1370→          This is impossible: `exact absurd hlt (by omega)`."
  1371→          exact absurd hlt (by omega)
  1372→
  1373→Conclusion "
  1374→You've now done case analysis on both `Fin 2` (Level 17) and `Fin 3`.
  1375→
  1376→The pattern scales: for `Fin n`, you get `n` valid cases and one
  1377→impossible case. Each additional `n` adds one more nesting level.
  1378→
  1379→For large `n`, this would be tedious. That's why the `fin_cases`
  1380→tactic (currently disabled) exists — it automates the entire case
  1381→split. You'll unlock it in a later world.
  1382→
  1383→You've now verified by case analysis that `Fin 0` has 0 elements,
  1384→`Fin 1` has 1, `Fin 2` has 2, and `Fin 3` has 3. The general fact —
  1385→`Fin n` has exactly `n` elements — will be formalized using
  1386→`Fintype.card` in the Cardinality world.
  1387→
  1388→This practice prepares you for the boss, where you'll combine case
  1389→analysis with other moves.
  1390→"

  1394→import Game.Metadata
  1395→
  1396→World "MeetFin"
  1397→Level 19
  1398→
  1399→Title "Modular Arithmetic"
  1400→
  1401→Introduction "
  1402→# Enrichment: Fin Has Arithmetic
  1403→
  1404→*This level is an enrichment aside — a mathematical surprise that
  1405→connects `Fin n` to a different area of mathematics. The main course
  1406→path resumes in the next level.*
  1407→
  1408→You've been treating `Fin n` as an index set — a collection of
  1409→positions `0, 1, ..., n-1`. But `Fin n` also supports **arithmetic**,
  1410→and it works differently from natural number arithmetic.
  1411→
  1412→What is `3 + 4` in `Fin 5`? On natural numbers, it's `7`. But `Fin 5`
  1413→only has elements `0, 1, 2, 3, 4` — there is no `7`. Addition on
  1414→`Fin n` **wraps around**: it takes the result modulo `n`.
  1415→
  1416→So `(3 : Fin 5) + (4 : Fin 5) = (2 : Fin 5)` because `7 mod 5 = 2`.
  1417→This is **modular arithmetic** — the arithmetic of clocks, cyclic
  1418→groups, and the integers mod `n`.
  1419→
  1420→**Your task**: Find two elements `a, b : Fin 5` such that
  1421→`a + b = 0` (their sum wraps around to zero) and their natural
  1422→number values sum to `5`.
  1423→
  1424→(Hint: on a 5-hour clock, which two different hours are diametrically
  1425→opposite — summing to a full rotation?)
  1426→"
  1427→
  1428→/-- Find two Fin 5 elements that sum to 0 modularly and to 5 naturally. -/
  1429→Statement : ∃ a b : Fin 5, a + b = 0 ∧ a.val + b.val = 5 := by
  1430→  Hint "Provide both witnesses with `use`.
  1431→  Which two numbers less than 5 sum to 5 as naturals and to 0 mod 5?"
  1432→  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use ⟨1, by omega⟩, ⟨4, by omega⟩`."
  1433→  use ⟨1, by omega⟩, ⟨4, by omega⟩
  1434→  Hint "Now prove both parts of the conjunction with `constructor`."
  1435→  constructor
  1436→  · Hint "The sum `1 + 4` in Fin 5 equals `5 mod 5 = 0`.
  1437→    Since this is a definitional computation, `rfl` closes it."
  1438→    rfl
  1439→  · Hint "The natural sum `1 + 4 = 5`. Since the values are concrete
  1440→    numbers, Lean computes the result and `rfl` closes it."
  1441→    rfl
  1442→
  1443→Conclusion "
  1444→Every nonzero element of `Fin 5` generates the whole group under
  1445→repeated addition — after exactly 5 steps, you return to 0. This
  1446→is because 5 is prime, making `Fin 5` a **cyclic group** where
  1447→every nonzero element is a generator.
  1448→
  1449→More examples of modular arithmetic in `Fin 5`:
  1450→- `(3 : Fin 5) + (4 : Fin 5) = 2` (because `7 mod 5 = 2`)
  1451→- `(2 : Fin 5) * (3 : Fin 5) = 1` (because `6 mod 5 = 1`)
  1452→- `(4 : Fin 5) + (1 : Fin 5) = 0` (because `5 mod 5 = 0`)
  1453→
  1454→Notice that both halves of the conjunction close by `rfl` — both
  1455→are *definitional computations*. The modular sum `1 + 4` reduces
  1456→to `0` in `Fin 5` because Lean computes `5 mod 5 = 0`. The natural
  1457→sum `1 + 4` reduces to `5` because Lean computes `1 + 4 = 5`.
  1458→The `constructor` tactic split the conjunction so each half could
  1459→be verified independently.
  1460→
  1461→This is the arithmetic of a **clock with `n` hours**: when you pass
  1462→the maximum, you wrap back to 0. Mathematically, `Fin n` with this
  1463→arithmetic is a model of the integers modulo `n` (written
  1464→$\\mathbb{Z}/n\\mathbb{Z}$).
  1465→
  1466→This course focuses on `Fin n` as a finite index type, not as a
  1467→number system. But this modular structure appears again sooner than
  1468→you might expect. In the Problem Set (PsetFin), you'll study the
  1469→cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`. That permutation
  1470→cycles `0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0` — exactly the same cyclic structure you
  1471→see here with addition modulo 5. The cyclic permutation and
  1472→modular addition are both examples of **cyclic groups**: structures
  1473→where repeated application of an operation eventually returns to
  1474→the starting point.
  1475→
  1476→When you study `Fintype.card` in the Cardinality world, the fact
  1477→that `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n` connects the type-theoretic size
  1478→of `Fin n` to this algebraic structure.
  1479→
  1480→Now back to our main path: using `Fin` as an index for concrete data.
  1481→"
  1482→

  1498→
  1499→A function `Fin n → α` is essentially an `n`-element tuple: it assigns

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 2` is either `0` or `1`.

Statement (x : Fin 2) : x = 0 ∨ x = 1 := by

  | mk v hlt =>
    Hint "Now `v : ℕ` and `hlt : v < 2`. Case-split on `v`:
    `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint "In this case `v = 0`, so `x` is the element `0`.
      Choose the left disjunct with `left`, then close with `rfl`."
      left
      rfl
    | succ n =>
      Hint "Now `v = n + 1`. Case-split once more on `n` to determine
      whether `v = 1` or `v ≥ 2`."
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint "Now `v = 1`. Choose the right disjunct with `right`,
        then close with `rfl`."
        right
        rfl
      | succ m =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = m + 2`, but `hlt` says `v < 2`.
        This is impossible — `exact absurd hlt (by omega)` closes it."
        exact absurd hlt (by omega)

Conclusion "
You've just performed **exhaustive case analysis** on `Fin 2`.

The strategy — which we'll call **Fin case analysis** — is:
1. **Destructure** the `Fin` element (Level 16)
2. **Case-split** on the natural number (nested `cases` as needed)
3. **Handle** each case: valid cases with `left`/`right` + `rfl`,
   impossible cases with `absurd` + `omega`

**Why does this work?** Exhaustive case analysis on `Fin n` terminates
because `.isLt` bounds the natural number. Each `cases` step peels
off one possible value (0, then 1, then 2, ...), and the `.isLt`
bound ensures that after `n` valid cases, the remaining case is
impossible — the value would need to be ≥ `n`, contradicting `v < n`.
This is the **Fin contradiction pattern** from Level 13 applied to the
impossible branch. This termination is what makes `Fin n` a *finite*
type in a proof-relevant sense: you can enumerate all elements and
handle each one. This idea will be formalized as `Fintype` in a later
world.

This strategy works for any `Fin n` by nesting enough `cases` on the
natural number. But imagine doing this for `Fin 100`: you'd need 100
branches, 99 of which are contradiction proofs. The `fin_cases` tactic
(currently disabled) automates all of this — it performs the case split
and handles the impossible branches automatically. You'll unlock it
later.

Fun fact: `Fin 2` is equivalent to `Bool` — both have exactly two
elements. Where `Bool` has `true` and `false`, `Fin 2` has `0` and `1`.

**A note on `absurd`**: You used `absurd` here for a *bound violation*
in case analysis — the value was too large for the bound. This is a
general tool you'll use in later worlds whenever you have contradictory
hypotheses. In the next level, you'll practice case analysis on `Fin 3`,
and after that you'll learn a different pattern (`intro h; cases h`)
for when *constructors themselves* don't match (like `0 = 1`).
"

/-- `absurd` closes a goal when you have contradictory hypotheses.

## Syntax
```
exact absurd h1 h2
```
where `h1 : P` and `h2 : ¬P` (or `h1 : ¬P` and `h2 : P`).

## When to use it
When you have a proposition and its negation in your hypotheses,
or when you can derive one of them from context.

## Example
```
-- hlt : v < 2
-- But v = m + 2, so v < 2 is impossible
exact absurd hlt (by omega)
```

## Note
`absurd` is the formal version of proof by contradiction:
if you can show both `P` and `¬P`, the current goal is closed.
-/
TacticDoc absurd

NewTactic absurd

World "MeetFin"
Level 18

Title "Case Analysis Practice"

Introduction "
# Practice: Case Analysis on Fin 3

In Level 17, you exhausted all cases of `Fin 2` (two valid, one
impossible). Now try `Fin 3` — the same strategy with one more valid
case.

**Why are some tactics disabled?** You might wonder why tactics like
`fin_cases`, `decide`, and `norm_num` are unavailable. These tactics
can automate case analysis and arithmetic in one step — which is
exactly why they're disabled here. The manual approach forces you to
understand *what* the automation does: destructure the `Fin` element,
split on the natural number, handle each valid case, and dismiss
impossible cases with `absurd`. Once you understand this structure,
you'll appreciate the automation when it's unlocked later. The
automation doesn't replace this understanding — it just lets you
skip the mechanical parts once you've mastered them.

The pattern is the same:
1. **Destructure**: `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`
2. **Case-split on `v`**: `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`
3. **Nest further** as needed
4. **Valid cases**: `left`/`right` + `rfl`
5. **Impossible cases**: `exact absurd hlt (by omega)`

For `Fin 3`, you'll have three valid cases (`v = 0, 1, 2`) and one
impossible case (`v ≥ 3`).

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 3` is `0`, `1`, or `2`.
"

/-- Every element of `Fin 3` is `0`, `1`, or `2`. -/
Statement (x : Fin 3) : x = 0 ∨ x = 1 ∨ x = 2 := by
  Hint "Start by destructuring: `cases x with | mk v hlt =>`"
  cases x with
  | mk v hlt =>
    Hint "Case-split on `v`: `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint "Here `v = 0`. Choose the left disjunct: `left` then `rfl`."
      left
      rfl
    | succ n =>
      Hint "Now `v = n + 1`. Case-split again on `n`."
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint "Here `v = 1`. Choose `right` then `left` then `rfl`."
        right
        left
        rfl
      | succ m =>
        Hint "Now `v = m + 2`. One more case split on `m`."
        cases m with
        | zero =>
          Hint "Here `v = 2`. Choose `right` then `right` then `rfl`."
          right
          right
          rfl
        | succ k =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hlt` says `v < 3`.
          This is impossible: `exact absurd hlt (by omega)`."
          exact absurd hlt (by omega)

Conclusion "
You've now done case analysis on both `Fin 2` (Level 17) and `Fin 3`.

The pattern scales: for `Fin n`, you get `n` valid cases and one
impossible case. Each additional `n` adds one more nesting level.

For large `n`, this would be tedious. That's why the `fin_cases`
tactic (currently disabled) exists — it automates the entire case
split. You'll unlock it in a later world.

You've now verified by case analysis that `Fin 0` has 0 elements,
`Fin 1` has 1, `Fin 2` has 2, and `Fin 3` has 3. The general fact —
`Fin n` has exactly `n` elements — will be formalized using
`Fintype.card` in the Cardinality world.

This practice prepares you for the boss, where you'll combine case
analysis with other moves.
"

World "MeetFin"
Level 19

Title "Modular Arithmetic"

Introduction "
# Enrichment: Fin Has Arithmetic

*This level is an enrichment aside — a mathematical surprise that
connects `Fin n` to a different area of mathematics. The main course
path resumes in the next level.*

You've been treating `Fin n` as an index set — a collection of
positions `0, 1, ..., n-1`. But `Fin n` also supports **arithmetic**,
and it works differently from natural number arithmetic.

What is `3 + 4` in `Fin 5`? On natural numbers, it's `7`. But `Fin 5`
only has elements `0, 1, 2, 3, 4` — there is no `7`. Addition on
`Fin n` **wraps around**: it takes the result modulo `n`.

So `(3 : Fin 5) + (4 : Fin 5) = (2 : Fin 5)` because `7 mod 5 = 2`.
This is **modular arithmetic** — the arithmetic of clocks, cyclic
groups, and the integers mod `n`.

**Your task**: Find two elements `a, b : Fin 5` such that
`a + b = 0` (their sum wraps around to zero) and their natural
number values sum to `5`.

(Hint: on a 5-hour clock, which two different hours are diametrically
opposite — summing to a full rotation?)
"

/-- Find two Fin 5 elements that sum to 0 modularly and to 5 naturally. -/
Statement : ∃ a b : Fin 5, a + b = 0 ∧ a.val + b.val = 5 := by
  Hint "Provide both witnesses with `use`.
  Which two numbers less than 5 sum to 5 as naturals and to 0 mod 5?"
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use ⟨1, by omega⟩, ⟨4, by omega⟩`."
  use ⟨1, by omega⟩, ⟨4, by omega⟩
  Hint "Now prove both parts of the conjunction with `constructor`."
  constructor
  · Hint "The sum `1 + 4` in Fin 5 equals `5 mod 5 = 0`.
    Since this is a definitional computation, `rfl` closes it."
    rfl
  · Hint "The natural sum `1 + 4 = 5`. Since the values are concrete
    numbers, Lean computes the result and `rfl` closes it."
    rfl

Conclusion "
Every nonzero element of `Fin 5` generates the whole group under
repeated addition — after exactly 5 steps, you return to 0. This
is because 5 is prime, making `Fin 5` a **cyclic group** where
every nonzero element is a generator.

More examples of modular arithmetic in `Fin 5`:
- `(3 : Fin 5) + (4 : Fin 5) = 2` (because `7 mod 5 = 2`)
- `(2 : Fin 5) * (3 : Fin 5) = 1` (because `6 mod 5 = 1`)
- `(4 : Fin 5) + (1 : Fin 5) = 0` (because `5 mod 5 = 0`)

Notice that both halves of the conjunction close by `rfl` — both
are *definitional computations*. The modular sum `1 + 4` reduces
to `0` in `Fin 5` because Lean computes `5 mod 5 = 0`. The natural
sum `1 + 4` reduces to `5` because Lean computes `1 + 4 = 5`.
The `constructor` tactic split the conjunction so each half could
be verified independently.

This is the arithmetic of a **clock with `n` hours**: when you pass
the maximum, you wrap back to 0. Mathematically, `Fin n` with this
arithmetic is a model of the integers modulo `n` (written
$\\mathbb{Z}/n\\mathbb{Z}$).

This course focuses on `Fin n` as a finite index type, not as a
number system. But this modular structure appears again sooner than
you might expect. In the Problem Set (PsetFin), you'll study the
cyclic permutation `![1, 2, 0] : Fin 3 -> Fin 3`. That permutation
cycles `0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 0` — exactly the same cyclic structure you
see here with addition modulo 5. The cyclic permutation and
modular addition are both examples of **cyclic groups**: structures
where repeated application of an operation eventually returns to
the starting point.

When you study `Fintype.card` in the Cardinality world, the fact
that `Fintype.card (Fin n) = n` connects the type-theoretic size
of `Fin n` to this algebraic structure.

Now back to our main path: using `Fin` as an index for concrete data.
"

Level 20

Title "Using Fin as an Index"

Introduction "
# Fin in Action: Indexing Concrete Data

So far, `Fin` has been an abstract mathematical object. But `Fin n`
exists to **index things** — vector entries, polygon vertices, tuple
positions.

A function `Fin n → α` is essentially an `n`-element tuple: it assigns

content.content:
1500→a value of type `α` to each index `0, 1, ..., n-1`. You'll meet tuples
  1501→formally in the FinTuples world, but let's preview the idea.
  1502→
  1503→**Key fact**: A function on `Fin 3` is completely determined by its
  1504→three values — `f 0`, `f 1`, and `f 2`. If two functions agree on
  1505→these three inputs, they agree everywhere. This is because `Fin 3`
  1506→has exactly three elements (as you proved in Level 18).
  1507→
  1508→**Your task**: Prove that two functions `f g : Fin 3 → ℕ` are
  1509→equal on all inputs if they agree at positions 0, 1, and 2.
  1510→
  1511→This requires case analysis: for each possible input `i : Fin 3`,
  1512→determine which of the three hypotheses closes the goal.
  1513→"
  1514→
  1515→/-- A function on Fin 3 is determined by its three values. -/
  1516→Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = g 0) (h1 : f 1 = g 1)
  1517→    (h2 : f 2 = g 2) : ∀ i : Fin 3, f i = g i := by
  1518→  Hint "Start by introducing `i`, then destructure it:
  1519→  `intro i; cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
  1520→  intro i
  1521→  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
  1522→  cases i with
  1523→  | mk v hlt =>
  1524→    Hint "Case-split on `v` to determine which position `i` is at.
  1525→    For each valid case (0, 1, 2), one of the three hypotheses
  1526→    closes the goal."
  1527→    cases v with
  1528→    | zero =>
  1529→      Hint "Here `i` is at position 0 (value `v = 0`).
  1530→      One of the three hypotheses `h0`, `h1`, `h2` matches.
  1531→      Use `exact h0`."
  1532→      exact h0
  1533→    | succ n =>
  1534→      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`."
  1535→      cases n with
  1536→      | zero =>
  1537→        Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 1. `exact h1` closes the goal."
  1538→        exact h1
  1539→      | succ m =>
  1540→        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `m`."
  1541→        cases m with
  1542→        | zero =>
  1543→          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 2. `exact h2` closes the goal."
  1544→          exact h2
  1545→        | succ k =>
  1546→          Hint (hidden := true) "Impossible case: `exact absurd hlt (by omega)`."
  1547→          exact absurd hlt (by omega)
  1548→
  1549→Conclusion "
  1550→This is the fundamental property of `Fin n` as an index type: since
  1551→`Fin n` has exactly `n` elements, any predicate on `Fin n` can be
  1552→verified by checking each element. A function `Fin n → α` is
  1553→determined by `n` values, and two such functions agree if and only
  1554→if they agree on all `n` inputs.
  1555→
  1556→This is how `Fin n` connects to the concept of an **n-tuple**: the
  1557→function `f : Fin 3 → ℕ` is just the triple `(f 0, f 1, f 2)`. Two
  1558→triples are equal when they agree position by position. You'll
  1559→formalize this connection in the FinTuples world.
  1560→
  1561→In the next level, you'll learn to prove that two `Fin` elements
  1562→are *not* equal — the inequality pattern.
  1563→"

  1567→import Game.Metadata
  1568→
  1569→World "MeetFin"
  1570→Level 21
  1571→
  1572→Title "Proving Inequality"
  1573→
  1574→Introduction "
  1575→# Proving Two Fin Elements Are Not Equal
  1576→
  1577→In Level 17, you used `absurd` when a bound was violated — the value
  1578→was too large but the bound said it must be small. Here the situation
  1579→is different: the *constructors themselves* don't match (like `0 = 1`),
  1580→and a simpler pattern handles this case.
  1581→
  1582→How do you prove `a ≠ b` for `Fin` elements? In Lean, `a ≠ b` is
  1583→defined as `a = b → False` — a function that takes an equality proof
  1584→and derives a contradiction.
  1585→
  1586→The strategy is:
  1587→1. `intro h` — assume `a = b` (giving `h : a = b`)
  1588→2. `cases h` — Lean checks if the constructors match; if the underlying
  1589→   values differ, it closes the goal automatically
  1590→
  1591→When `h : ⟨a, _⟩ = ⟨b, _⟩` and `a ≠ b` as natural numbers, `cases h`
  1592→performs *injection* — it sees the values inside the constructors don't
  1593→match and derives a contradiction. This is the same idea as `Fin.ext_iff`
  1594→in reverse: if the values differ, the elements can't be equal.
  1595→
  1596→**Your task**: Prove that `0` and `1` are different elements of `Fin 3`.
  1597→"
  1598→
  1599→/-- `0` and `1` are distinct elements of `Fin 3`. -/
  1600→Statement : (0 : Fin 3) ≠ 1 := by
  1601→  Hint "Remember, `≠` means `= → False`. Start with `intro h`
  1602→  to assume they are equal."
  1603→  intro h
  1604→  Hint "Now `{h}` says these `Fin` elements are equal. But their
  1605→  underlying values are `0` and `1` — different natural numbers.
  1606→  `cases h` performs injection and sees the contradiction."
  1607→  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases h`."
  1608→  cases h
  1609→
  1610→Conclusion "
  1611→The pattern for proving `a ≠ b` on `Fin` elements is:
  1612→```
  1613→intro h
  1614→cases h
  1615→```
  1616→
  1617→This is called **constructor discrimination**: when `cases` examines
  1618→an equality `h : ⟨a, _⟩ = ⟨b, _⟩`, it performs *injection* (extracts
  1619→that `a = b`) and then checks whether this is consistent. When `a`
  1620→and `b` are distinct constructors (like `Nat.zero` and `Nat.succ _`),
  1621→the equality is impossible, and the goal closes automatically. This
  1622→pattern works for any type with distinct constructors — `Nat`, `Bool`,
  1623→`Fin`, etc. — not just `Fin`.
  1624→
  1625→You could also prove this using `Fin.ext_iff`: `intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`.
  1626→This reduces the `Fin` equality to `0 = 1` at the `ℕ` level, then
  1627→`omega` sees the contradiction. Both approaches work — here's when
  1628→to choose each:
  1629→
  1630→- **`intro h; cases h`** — use when both sides are **concrete literals**
  1631→  (like `0` and `1`). The constructor discrimination is automatic.
  1632→- **`intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`** — use when either side
  1633→  involves a **variable** or **arithmetic expression** (like `i` or
  1634→  `i + 1`). The `cases` approach won't fire on variable expressions,
  1635→  but reducing to values and using `omega` always works.
  1636→
  1637→This is the discrimination step you'll need in the boss.
  1638→
  1639→**Looking ahead**: The fact that Lean can *computationally* determine
  1640→whether two `Fin` elements are equal is called **decidable equality**.
  1641→The `intro h; cases h` pattern works because Lean's kernel can evaluate
  1642→whether the underlying constructors match. This property — `DecidableEq
  1643→(Fin n)` — is what will make `Finset (Fin n)` possible in Phase 2: a
  1644→finite set needs to check for duplicates, and decidable equality is what
  1645→makes that check computable.
  1646→"
  1647→

  1654→Title "Existential Witnesses"
  1655→
  1656→Introduction "
  1657→# Providing a Fin Element as a Witness
  1658→
  1659→In Level 1, you constructed a `Fin` element with `exact ⟨k, by omega⟩`.
  1660→But what if the goal asks you to prove that *some* element exists
  1661→with a property? Then you need `use` to provide the witness.
  1662→
  1663→When the goal is `∃ x : Fin n, P x`, the tactic `use ⟨k, by omega⟩`
  1664→provides a specific `Fin n` element as the witness, then asks you
  1665→to prove `P` holds for that element.
  1666→
  1667→This combines two skills you already know:
  1668→- `use` from the NNG4 prerequisites (providing existential witnesses)
  1669→- `⟨k, by omega⟩` from Level 1 (constructing `Fin` elements)
  1670→
  1671→**Your task**: Find a `Fin 5` element whose value is `3`.
  1672→"
  1673→
  1674→/-- There exists an element of `Fin 5` with value `3`. -/
  1675→Statement : ∃ x : Fin 5, x.val = 3 := by
  1676→  Hint "Provide the witness with `use ⟨3, by omega⟩`. This constructs
  1677→  the element `3 : Fin 5` and provides it as the existential witness.
  1678→  The remaining goal `3 = 3` closes automatically."
  1679→  Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨3, by omega⟩` provides the witness and
  1680→  Lean closes `3 = 3` by reflexivity."
  1681→  use ⟨3, by omega⟩
  1682→
  1683→Conclusion "
  1684→The pattern `use ⟨k, by omega⟩` provides a `Fin` element as an
  1685→existential witness. This is simply the combination of two moves:
  1686→
  1687→| Move | From |
  1688→|---|---|
  1689→| `use expr` — provide a witness | NNG4 prerequisites |
  1690→| `⟨k, by omega⟩` — construct a Fin element | Level 1 |
  1691→
  1692→You'll use this combination in the boss.
  1693→"

  1698→World "MeetFin"
  1699→Level 23
  1700→
  1701→Title "Boss: Fin Fundamentals"
  1702→
  1703→Introduction "
  1704→# Boss: Putting It All Together
  1705→
  1706→Time to combine everything you've learned in a two-part challenge.
  1707→
  1708→**Part 1** — *Transitivity through extensionality*: If `x` and `y`
  1709→have the same value, and `y = z` as Fin elements, then `x = z`. This
  1710→combines `ext` on the goal (Level 15) with `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`
  1711→on hypotheses (Level 7).
  1712→
  1713→**Part 2** — *Non-triviality*: Fin 3 has more than one element.
  1714→This requires exhaustive case analysis (Levels 16–18), existential
  1715→witnesses (Level 22), inequality proving (Level 21), and the Fin
  1716→contradiction pattern (Level 13) for the impossible branch.
  1717→
  1718→The boss tests whether you can assemble these moves into a
  1719→coherent proof. No single level taught all of this at once.
  1720→"
  1721→
  1722→/-- Fin equality is transitive through values, and Fin 3 is non-trivial. -/
  1723→Statement : (∀ x y z : Fin 3, x.val = y.val → y = z → x = z) ∧
  1724→            (∀ x : Fin 3, ∃ y : Fin 3, x ≠ y) := by
  1725→  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it
  1726→  into two sub-goals."
  1727→  constructor
  1728→  -- Part 1: transitivity through extensionality
  1729→  · Hint "Start with `intro x y z h1 h2`."
  1730→    intro x y z h1 h2
  1731→    Hint "You need to show `x = z`. Use `ext` to convert this to
  1732→    a value equality `x.val = z.val`."
  1733→    ext
  1734→    Hint "Now you need `x.val = z.val`. Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2`
  1735→    to extract `y.val = z.val` from `{h2}`."
  1736→    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2
  1737→    Hint "Now `{h1}` says `x.val = y.val` and `{h2}` says
  1738→    `y.val = z.val`. `omega` can chain these."
  1739→    omega
  1740→  -- Part 2: non-triviality via exhaustive case analysis
  1741→  · Hint "Perform exhaustive case analysis: destructure `x` with
  1742→    `cases`, then split on the value. For each valid case (0, 1, 2),
  1743→    choose a different `Fin 3` element as witness and prove inequality
  1744→    with `intro h; cases h`. The impossible case (value >= 3) closes
  1745→    with `absurd`."
  1746→    intro x
  1747→    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure `x`: `cases x with | mk v hv =>`"
  1748→    cases x with
  1749→    | mk v hv =>
  1750→      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `v`:
  1751→      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
  1752→      cases v with
  1753→      | zero =>
  1754→        Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `0`. Provide `1` as witness:
  1755→        `use ⟨1, by omega⟩`, then prove `0 ≠ 1` with `intro h; cases h`."
  1756→        use ⟨1, by omega⟩
  1757→        intro h
  1758→        cases h
  1759→      | succ n =>
  1760→        Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `n + 1`. Split on `n`."
  1761→        cases n with
  1762→        | zero =>
  1763→          Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `1`. Use `⟨0, by omega⟩`
  1764→          as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
  1765→          use ⟨0, by omega⟩
  1766→          intro h
  1767→          cases h
  1768→        | succ m =>
  1769→          Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `m + 2`. Split on `m`."
  1770→          cases m with
  1771→          | zero =>
  1772→            Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `2`. Use `⟨0, by omega⟩`
  1773→            as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
  1774→            use ⟨0, by omega⟩
  1775→            intro h
  1776→            cases h
  1777→          | succ k =>
  1778→            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
  1779→            This is impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
  1780→            exact absurd hv (by omega)
  1781→
  1782→Conclusion "
  1783→Congratulations — you've completed **Meet Fin**!
  1784→
  1785→You now have these core moves for working with `Fin n`:
  1786→
  1787→| Move | Tool | Learned in |
  1788→|---|---|---|
  1789→| Construct a `Fin n` element | `⟨k, by omega⟩` | Level 1 |
  1790→| Extract the number / coercion | `.val`, `.isLt`, `↑i` | Level 2 |
  1791→| Upper bound proof | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 3 |
  1792→| Combine bounds from multiple elements | multiple `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 4 |
  1793→| Prove `Fin` elements equal | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` or `ext` | Levels 5, 15 |
  1794→| Rewrite hypotheses | `rw [...] at h` | Levels 7–8 |
  1795→| Derive Fin.eta | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; rfl` | Level 10 |

file.content:
a value of type `α` to each index `0, 1, ..., n-1`. You'll meet tuples
formally in the FinTuples world, but let's preview the idea.

**Key fact**: A function on `Fin 3` is completely determined by its
three values — `f 0`, `f 1`, and `f 2`. If two functions agree on
these three inputs, they agree everywhere. This is because `Fin 3`
has exactly three elements (as you proved in Level 18).

**Your task**: Prove that two functions `f g : Fin 3 → ℕ` are
equal on all inputs if they agree at positions 0, 1, and 2.

This requires case analysis: for each possible input `i : Fin 3`,
determine which of the three hypotheses closes the goal.
"

/-- A function on Fin 3 is determined by its three values. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = g 0) (h1 : f 1 = g 1)
    (h2 : f 2 = g 2) : ∀ i : Fin 3, f i = g i := by
  Hint "Start by introducing `i`, then destructure it:
  `intro i; cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
  intro i
  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
  cases i with
  | mk v hlt =>
    Hint "Case-split on `v` to determine which position `i` is at.
    For each valid case (0, 1, 2), one of the three hypotheses
    closes the goal."
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint "Here `i` is at position 0 (value `v = 0`).
      One of the three hypotheses `h0`, `h1`, `h2` matches.
      Use `exact h0`."
      exact h0
    | succ n =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`."
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 1. `exact h1` closes the goal."
        exact h1
      | succ m =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `m`."
        cases m with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 2. `exact h2` closes the goal."
          exact h2
        | succ k =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Impossible case: `exact absurd hlt (by omega)`."
          exact absurd hlt (by omega)

Conclusion "
This is the fundamental property of `Fin n` as an index type: since
`Fin n` has exactly `n` elements, any predicate on `Fin n` can be
verified by checking each element. A function `Fin n → α` is
determined by `n` values, and two such functions agree if and only
if they agree on all `n` inputs.

This is how `Fin n` connects to the concept of an **n-tuple**: the
function `f : Fin 3 → ℕ` is just the triple `(f 0, f 1, f 2)`. Two
triples are equal when they agree position by position. You'll
formalize this connection in the FinTuples world.

In the next level, you'll learn to prove that two `Fin` elements
are *not* equal — the inequality pattern.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 21

Title "Proving Inequality"

Introduction "
# Proving Two Fin Elements Are Not Equal

In Level 17, you used `absurd` when a bound was violated — the value
was too large but the bound said it must be small. Here the situation
is different: the *constructors themselves* don't match (like `0 = 1`),
and a simpler pattern handles this case.

How do you prove `a ≠ b` for `Fin` elements? In Lean, `a ≠ b` is
defined as `a = b → False` — a function that takes an equality proof
and derives a contradiction.

The strategy is:
1. `intro h` — assume `a = b` (giving `h : a = b`)
2. `cases h` — Lean checks if the constructors match; if the underlying
   values differ, it closes the goal automatically

When `h : ⟨a, _⟩ = ⟨b, _⟩` and `a ≠ b` as natural numbers, `cases h`
performs *injection* — it sees the values inside the constructors don't
match and derives a contradiction. This is the same idea as `Fin.ext_iff`
in reverse: if the values differ, the elements can't be equal.

**Your task**: Prove that `0` and `1` are different elements of `Fin 3`.
"

/-- `0` and `1` are distinct elements of `Fin 3`. -/
Statement : (0 : Fin 3) ≠ 1 := by
  Hint "Remember, `≠` means `= → False`. Start with `intro h`
  to assume they are equal."
  intro h
  Hint "Now `{h}` says these `Fin` elements are equal. But their
  underlying values are `0` and `1` — different natural numbers.
  `cases h` performs injection and sees the contradiction."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases h`."
  cases h

Conclusion "
The pattern for proving `a ≠ b` on `Fin` elements is:
```
intro h
cases h
```

This is called **constructor discrimination**: when `cases` examines
an equality `h : ⟨a, _⟩ = ⟨b, _⟩`, it performs *injection* (extracts
that `a = b`) and then checks whether this is consistent. When `a`
and `b` are distinct constructors (like `Nat.zero` and `Nat.succ _`),
the equality is impossible, and the goal closes automatically. This
pattern works for any type with distinct constructors — `Nat`, `Bool`,
`Fin`, etc. — not just `Fin`.

You could also prove this using `Fin.ext_iff`: `intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`.
This reduces the `Fin` equality to `0 = 1` at the `ℕ` level, then
`omega` sees the contradiction. Both approaches work — here's when
to choose each:

- **`intro h; cases h`** — use when both sides are **concrete literals**
  (like `0` and `1`). The constructor discrimination is automatic.
- **`intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`** — use when either side
  involves a **variable** or **arithmetic expression** (like `i` or
  `i + 1`). The `cases` approach won't fire on variable expressions,
  but reducing to values and using `omega` always works.

This is the discrimination step you'll need in the boss.

**Looking ahead**: The fact that Lean can *computationally* determine
whether two `Fin` elements are equal is called **decidable equality**.
The `intro h; cases h` pattern works because Lean's kernel can evaluate
whether the underlying constructors match. This property — `DecidableEq
(Fin n)` — is what will make `Finset (Fin n)` possible in Phase 2: a
finite set needs to check for duplicates, and decidable equality is what
makes that check computable.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 22

Title "Existential Witnesses"

Introduction "
# Providing a Fin Element as a Witness

In Level 1, you constructed a `Fin` element with `exact ⟨k, by omega⟩`.
But what if the goal asks you to prove that *some* element exists
with a property? Then you need `use` to provide the witness.

When the goal is `∃ x : Fin n, P x`, the tactic `use ⟨k, by omega⟩`
provides a specific `Fin n` element as the witness, then asks you
to prove `P` holds for that element.

This combines two skills you already know:
- `use` from the NNG4 prerequisites (providing existential witnesses)
- `⟨k, by omega⟩` from Level 1 (constructing `Fin` elements)

**Your task**: Find a `Fin 5` element whose value is `3`.
"

/-- There exists an element of `Fin 5` with value `3`. -/
Statement : ∃ x : Fin 5, x.val = 3 := by
  Hint "Provide the witness with `use ⟨3, by omega⟩`. This constructs
  the element `3 : Fin 5` and provides it as the existential witness.
  The remaining goal `3 = 3` closes automatically."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`use ⟨3, by omega⟩` provides the witness and
  Lean closes `3 = 3` by reflexivity."
  use ⟨3, by omega⟩

Conclusion "
The pattern `use ⟨k, by omega⟩` provides a `Fin` element as an
existential witness. This is simply the combination of two moves:

| Move | From |
|---|---|
| `use expr` — provide a witness | NNG4 prerequisites |
| `⟨k, by omega⟩` — construct a Fin element | Level 1 |

You'll use this combination in the boss.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 23

Title "Boss: Fin Fundamentals"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting It All Together

Time to combine everything you've learned in a two-part challenge.

**Part 1** — *Transitivity through extensionality*: If `x` and `y`
have the same value, and `y = z` as Fin elements, then `x = z`. This
combines `ext` on the goal (Level 15) with `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`
on hypotheses (Level 7).

**Part 2** — *Non-triviality*: Fin 3 has more than one element.
This requires exhaustive case analysis (Levels 16–18), existential
witnesses (Level 22), inequality proving (Level 21), and the Fin
contradiction pattern (Level 13) for the impossible branch.

The boss tests whether you can assemble these moves into a
coherent proof. No single level taught all of this at once.
"

/-- Fin equality is transitive through values, and Fin 3 is non-trivial. -/
Statement : (∀ x y z : Fin 3, x.val = y.val → y = z → x = z) ∧
            (∀ x : Fin 3, ∃ y : Fin 3, x ≠ y) := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it
  into two sub-goals."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: transitivity through extensionality
  · Hint "Start with `intro x y z h1 h2`."
    intro x y z h1 h2
    Hint "You need to show `x = z`. Use `ext` to convert this to
    a value equality `x.val = z.val`."
    ext
    Hint "Now you need `x.val = z.val`. Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2`
    to extract `y.val = z.val` from `{h2}`."
    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2
    Hint "Now `{h1}` says `x.val = y.val` and `{h2}` says
    `y.val = z.val`. `omega` can chain these."
    omega
  -- Part 2: non-triviality via exhaustive case analysis
  · Hint "Perform exhaustive case analysis: destructure `x` with
    `cases`, then split on the value. For each valid case (0, 1, 2),
    choose a different `Fin 3` element as witness and prove inequality
    with `intro h; cases h`. The impossible case (value >= 3) closes
    with `absurd`."
    intro x
    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure `x`: `cases x with | mk v hv =>`"
    cases x with
    | mk v hv =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `v`:
      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
      cases v with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `0`. Provide `1` as witness:
        `use ⟨1, by omega⟩`, then prove `0 ≠ 1` with `intro h; cases h`."
        use ⟨1, by omega⟩
        intro h
        cases h
      | succ n =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `n + 1`. Split on `n`."
        cases n with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `1`. Use `⟨0, by omega⟩`
          as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
          use ⟨0, by omega⟩
          intro h
          cases h
        | succ m =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `m + 2`. Split on `m`."
          cases m with
          | zero =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `2`. Use `⟨0, by omega⟩`
            as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
            use ⟨0, by omega⟩
            intro h
            cases h
          | succ k =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
            This is impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
            exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
Congratulations — you've completed **Meet Fin**!

You now have these core moves for working with `Fin n`:

| Move | Tool | Learned in |
|---|---|---|
| Construct a `Fin n` element | `⟨k, by omega⟩` | Level 1 |
| Extract the number / coercion | `.val`, `.isLt`, `↑i` | Level 2 |
| Upper bound proof | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 3 |
| Combine bounds from multiple elements | multiple `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 4 |
| Prove `Fin` elements equal | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` or `ext` | Levels 5, 15 |
| Rewrite hypotheses | `rw [...] at h` | Levels 7–8 |
| Derive Fin.eta | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; rfl` | Level 10 |
| Proof irrelevance | `Fin.ext_iff` + `omega` | Level 11 |
| General `Fin (n+1)` reasoning | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 12 |
| Fin contradiction pattern | `.isLt` + `omega` on empty/impossible | Level 13 |
| Handle `Fin 1` (singleton) | `ext` + `omega` | Level 15 |

1→import Game.Metadata
     2→
     3→World "FinNavigation"
     4→Level 1
     5→
     6→Title "The Last Element"
     7→
     8→Introduction "
     9→# Fin.last: The Boundary Element
    10→
    11→In the previous world, you learned that `Fin n` contains the numbers
    12→$0, 1, \\ldots, n-1$. But how do you name the *largest* element?
    13→
    14→For `Fin (n+1)`, the largest element has value `n`. Lean gives it a
    15→name: **`Fin.last n`**.
    16→
    17→`Fin.last n` lives in `Fin (n+1)` — not `Fin n` — because `n` is
    18→less than `n+1` but not less than `n`.
    19→
    20→The theorem **`Fin.val_last`** extracts its value:
    21→$$\\texttt{(Fin.last } n\\texttt{).val} = n$$
    22→
    23→**Your task**: Show that `Fin.last 4` has value `4`.
    24→"
    25→
    26→/-- `Fin.last 4` has value 4. -/
    27→Statement : (Fin.last 4).val = 4 := by
    28→  Hint "Use `exact Fin.val_last 4` to close this goal.
    29→  (Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here.
    30→  But `Fin.val_last` is the general tool you'll need when `n`
    31→  is a variable.)"
    32→  exact Fin.val_last 4
    33→
    34→Conclusion "
    35→`Fin.val_last n` tells you that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.
    36→
    37→This is the analog of saying: the largest element of
    38→$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$ is $n$. In Lean, you need `Fin.val_last`
    39→to access this fact because `Fin.last` is an opaque term —
    40→the type system doesn't automatically compute its value.
    41→
    42→In the next level, you'll use `Fin.val_last` as a **rewrite** tool
    43→inside a larger proof.
    44→"
    45→
    46→/-- `Fin.last n` is the largest element of `Fin (n + 1)`.
    47→Its underlying value is `n`.
    48→
    49→## Construction
    50→`Fin.last n : Fin (n + 1)`
    51→
    52→## Example
    53→`Fin.last 4 : Fin 5` — the element with value 4.
    54→
    55→## Value
    56→`(Fin.last n).val = n` (see `Fin.val_last`).
    57→-/
    58→DefinitionDoc Fin.last as "Fin.last"
    59→
    60→/-- `Fin.val_last n` states that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.
    61→
    62→## Syntax
    63→```
    64→rw [Fin.val_last]    -- rewrites (Fin.last n).val to n
    65→exact Fin.val_last n -- closes a goal (Fin.last n).val = n
    66→```
    67→
    68→## When to use it
    69→When you need to compute or simplify the value of `Fin.last`.
    70→
    71→## Example
    72→```
    73→-- Goal: (Fin.last 4).val = 4
    74→exact Fin.val_last 4
    75→```
    76→-/
    77→TheoremDoc Fin.val_last as "Fin.val_last" in "Fin"
    78→
    79→NewDefinition Fin.last
    80→NewTheorem Fin.val_last
    81→

    84→import Game.Metadata
    85→
    86→World "FinNavigation"
    87→Level 2
    88→
    89→Title "Last is the Maximum"
    90→
    91→Introduction "
    92→# Using Fin.val_last as a Rewrite
    93→
    94→`Fin.val_last` isn't just for computing concrete values — it's a
    95→**rewrite** tool. When you have `(Fin.last n).val` somewhere in
    96→your goal, `rw [Fin.val_last]` replaces it with `n`, often
    97→unlocking `omega` to finish the job.
    98→
    99→**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 5` has value at
   100→most `(Fin.last 4).val`.
   101→
   102→Strategy:
   103→1. `intro i` to name the element
   104→2. `rw [Fin.val_last]` to simplify the right side to `4`
   105→3. `omega` to close `i.val ≤ 4` (since `i.val < 5`)
   106→"
   107→
   108→/-- Every element of `Fin 5` has value at most `(Fin.last 4).val`. -/
   109→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 5, i.val ≤ (Fin.last 4).val := by
   110→  Hint "Start with `intro i` to name the element."
   111→  intro i
   112→  Hint "Now `rw [Fin.val_last]` converts `(Fin.last 4).val` to `4`."
   113→  rw [Fin.val_last]
   114→  Hint "The goal is `i.val ≤ 4`. Since `i : Fin 5`, you know
   115→  `i.val < 5`, so `i.val ≤ 4`. `omega` handles this."
   116→  omega
   117→
   118→Conclusion "
   119→The **rewrite-then-omega** pattern will be your main tool in this
   120→world:
   121→
   122→1. Use a val lemma (`Fin.val_last`, or the ones you'll learn next)
   123→   to simplify `Fin` expressions to plain natural numbers
   124→2. Use `omega` to close the resulting arithmetic goal
   125→
   126→Why is `Fin.last` the maximum? Because every `i : Fin (n+1)`
   127→satisfies `i.val < n+1`, which means `i.val ≤ n`. And
   128→`(Fin.last n).val = n`. So `i.val ≤ (Fin.last n).val`.
   129→
   130→In plain math: $\\forall\\, i \\in \\{0, \\ldots, n\\},\\; i \\leq n$.
   131→"
   132→

   134→DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos Fin.le_last
   135→import Game.Metadata
   136→
   137→World "FinNavigation"
   138→Level 3
   139→
   140→Title "Embedding with castSucc"
   141→
   142→Introduction "
   143→# Fin.castSucc: Same Value, Bigger Type
   144→
   145→What if you have an element of `Fin n` and need it in `Fin (n+1)`?
   146→
   147→**`Fin.castSucc`** does exactly this: it takes `i : Fin n` and
   148→returns `i.castSucc : Fin (n+1)` with the **same value**.
   149→
   150→For example, if `i : Fin 3` has value `2`, then
   151→`i.castSucc : Fin 4` also has value `2`. The number doesn't
   152→change — only the type's upper bound increases.
   153→
   154→The theorem **`Fin.val_castSucc`** makes this precise:
   155→$$\\texttt{i.castSucc.val} = \\texttt{i.val}$$
   156→
   157→Why does this work? If `i.val < n`, then certainly `i.val < n+1`.
   158→So the same number satisfies the stricter bound.
   159→
   160→**Your task**: Prove that `castSucc` preserves the value.
   161→"
   162→
   163→/-- `castSucc` preserves the underlying value. -/
   164→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.castSucc).val = i.val := by
   165→  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_castSucc i`."
   166→  intro i
   167→  exact Fin.val_castSucc i
   168→
   169→Conclusion "
   170→`Fin.castSucc` is an **embedding**: it maps `Fin n` into
   171→`Fin (n+1)` without changing any values. Think of it as
   172→*widening the frame* — the elements stay in place, but the
   173→type now has room for one more element at the top.
   174→
   175→**Why not automatic coercion?** You might wonder why Lean doesn't
   176→just automatically treat `Fin n` elements as `Fin (n+1)` elements.
   177→The reason: Lean's type system doesn't have a built-in coercion
   178→from `Fin n` to `Fin m` for arbitrary `n <= m`. Each embedding must
   179→be explicit, and `castSucc` is the standard one for the `n -> n+1`
   180→step. This explicitness prevents silent type-widening bugs.
   181→
   182→(Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here —
   183→just as it did for `Fin.val_last` in Level 1. But
   184→`Fin.val_castSucc` is the tool you'll need when `n` is a
   185→variable, so practice using the named lemma.)
   186→
   187→The element that occupies that new top position is `Fin.last n`
   188→(from Level 1). So `Fin (n+1)` consists of:
   189→- The images of `Fin.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$)
   190→- `Fin.last n` (value $n$)
   191→
   192→You'll prove this decomposition in the boss level.
   193→
   194→In plain math: the inclusion $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\hookrightarrow
   195→\\{0, \\ldots, n\\}$ is the identity on elements.
   196→"
   197→
   198→/-- `Fin.castSucc` embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` by keeping
   199→the same value but relaxing the upper bound.
   200→
   201→## Syntax
   202→```
   203→i.castSucc      -- dot notation
   204→Fin.castSucc i  -- explicit
   205→```
   206→
   207→## Type
   208→`Fin.castSucc : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`
   209→
   210→## Value
   211→`i.castSucc.val = i.val` (see `Fin.val_castSucc`)
   212→
   213→## When to use it
   214→When you need to treat an element of `Fin n` as an element of
   215→`Fin (n + 1)` without changing its value.
   216→-/
   217→DefinitionDoc Fin.castSucc as "Fin.castSucc"
   218→
   219→/-- `Fin.val_castSucc i` states that `i.castSucc.val = i.val`.
   220→
   221→## Syntax
   222→```
   223→rw [Fin.val_castSucc]      -- rewrites i.castSucc.val to i.val
   224→exact Fin.val_castSucc i   -- closes the goal directly
   225→```
   226→
   227→## When to use it
   228→When you need to simplify `castSucc` to the underlying value.
   229→-/
   230→TheoremDoc Fin.val_castSucc as "Fin.val_castSucc" in "Fin"
   231→
   232→NewDefinition Fin.castSucc
   233→NewTheorem Fin.val_castSucc
   234→

   236→DisabledTheorem Fin.castSucc_lt_succ Fin.succ_pos
   237→import Game.Metadata
   238→
   239→World "FinNavigation"
   240→Level 4
   241→
   242→Title "Moving Forward: Fin.succ"
   243→
   244→Introduction "
   245→# Fin.succ: The Next Element
   246→
   247→There's a second way to go from `Fin n` to `Fin (n+1)`:
   248→**`Fin.succ`**, which **increments** the value by 1.
   249→
   250→If `i : Fin n` has value `k`, then `i.succ : Fin (n+1)` has
   251→value `k + 1`.
   252→
   253→Compare with `castSucc`:
   254→- `i.castSucc.val = i.val` (same value)
   255→- `i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (value plus one)
   256→
   257→Both send `Fin n → Fin (n+1)`, but they land at different
   258→positions. `castSucc` keeps you in place; `succ` moves you
   259→forward by one.
   260→
   261→The theorem **`Fin.val_succ`** states:
   262→$$\\texttt{i.succ.val} = \\texttt{i.val} + 1$$
   263→
   264→**Your task**: Prove this value relationship.
   265→"
   266→
   267→/-- `Fin.succ` increments the underlying value by 1. -/
   268→Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.succ).val = i.val + 1 := by
   269→  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_succ i`."
   270→  intro i
   271→  exact Fin.val_succ i
   272→
   273→Conclusion "
   274→(As with `Fin.val_last` and `Fin.val_castSucc`, `rfl` also works
   275→here since the definition is transparent. But `Fin.val_succ` is
   276→the rewrite tool you'll need when `n` is a variable.)
   277→
   278→You now have the three navigation functions and their val lemmas:
   279→
   280→| Function | Type | Value |
   281→|---|---|---|
   282→| `Fin.last n` | `Fin (n+1)` | `n` |
   283→| `i.castSucc` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val` (unchanged) |
   284→| `i.succ` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val + 1` |
   285→

   287→`Fin` navigation into plain arithmetic. The strategy for proving

   289→

   291→relationship between `castSucc` and `succ`.

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 1

Title "The Last Element"

Introduction "
# Fin.last: The Boundary Element

In the previous world, you learned that `Fin n` contains the numbers
$0, 1, \\ldots, n-1$. But how do you name the *largest* element?

For `Fin (n+1)`, the largest element has value `n`. Lean gives it a
name: **`Fin.last n`**.

`Fin.last n` lives in `Fin (n+1)` — not `Fin n` — because `n` is
less than `n+1` but not less than `n`.

The theorem **`Fin.val_last`** extracts its value:
$$\\texttt{(Fin.last } n\\texttt{).val} = n$$

**Your task**: Show that `Fin.last 4` has value `4`.
"

/-- `Fin.last 4` has value 4. -/
Statement : (Fin.last 4).val = 4 := by
  Hint "Use `exact Fin.val_last 4` to close this goal.
  (Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here.
  But `Fin.val_last` is the general tool you'll need when `n`
  is a variable.)"
  exact Fin.val_last 4

Conclusion "
`Fin.val_last n` tells you that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.

This is the analog of saying: the largest element of
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$ is $n$. In Lean, you need `Fin.val_last`
to access this fact because `Fin.last` is an opaque term —
the type system doesn't automatically compute its value.

In the next level, you'll use `Fin.val_last` as a **rewrite** tool
inside a larger proof.
"

/-- `Fin.last n` is the largest element of `Fin (n + 1)`.
Its underlying value is `n`.

## Construction
`Fin.last n : Fin (n + 1)`

## Example
`Fin.last 4 : Fin 5` — the element with value 4.

## Value
`(Fin.last n).val = n` (see `Fin.val_last`).
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.last as "Fin.last"

/-- `Fin.val_last n` states that `(Fin.last n).val = n`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_last]    -- rewrites (Fin.last n).val to n
exact Fin.val_last n -- closes a goal (Fin.last n).val = n
```

## When to use it
When you need to compute or simplify the value of `Fin.last`.

## Example
```
-- Goal: (Fin.last 4).val = 4
exact Fin.val_last 4
```
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_last as "Fin.val_last" in "Fin"

NewDefinition Fin.last
NewTheorem Fin.val_last

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 2

Title "Last is the Maximum"

Introduction "
# Using Fin.val_last as a Rewrite

`Fin.val_last` isn't just for computing concrete values — it's a
**rewrite** tool. When you have `(Fin.last n).val` somewhere in
your goal, `rw [Fin.val_last]` replaces it with `n`, often
unlocking `omega` to finish the job.

**Your task**: Prove that every element of `Fin 5` has value at
most `(Fin.last 4).val`.

Strategy:
1. `intro i` to name the element
2. `rw [Fin.val_last]` to simplify the right side to `4`
3. `omega` to close `i.val ≤ 4` (since `i.val < 5`)
"

/-- Every element of `Fin 5` has value at most `(Fin.last 4).val`. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 5, i.val ≤ (Fin.last 4).val := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i` to name the element."
  intro i
  Hint "Now `rw [Fin.val_last]` converts `(Fin.last 4).val` to `4`."
  rw [Fin.val_last]
  Hint "The goal is `i.val ≤ 4`. Since `i : Fin 5`, you know
  `i.val < 5`, so `i.val ≤ 4`. `omega` handles this."
  omega

Conclusion "
The **rewrite-then-omega** pattern will be your main tool in this
world:

1. Use a val lemma (`Fin.val_last`, or the ones you'll learn next)
   to simplify `Fin` expressions to plain natural numbers
2. Use `omega` to close the resulting arithmetic goal

Why is `Fin.last` the maximum? Because every `i : Fin (n+1)`
satisfies `i.val < n+1`, which means `i.val ≤ n`. And
`(Fin.last n).val = n`. So `i.val ≤ (Fin.last n).val`.

In plain math: $\\forall\\, i \\in \\{0, \\ldots, n\\},\\; i \\leq n$.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 3

Title "Embedding with castSucc"

Introduction "
# Fin.castSucc: Same Value, Bigger Type

What if you have an element of `Fin n` and need it in `Fin (n+1)`?

**`Fin.castSucc`** does exactly this: it takes `i : Fin n` and
returns `i.castSucc : Fin (n+1)` with the **same value**.

For example, if `i : Fin 3` has value `2`, then
`i.castSucc : Fin 4` also has value `2`. The number doesn't
change — only the type's upper bound increases.

The theorem **`Fin.val_castSucc`** makes this precise:
$$\\texttt{i.castSucc.val} = \\texttt{i.val}$$

Why does this work? If `i.val < n`, then certainly `i.val < n+1`.
So the same number satisfies the stricter bound.

**Your task**: Prove that `castSucc` preserves the value.
"

/-- `castSucc` preserves the underlying value. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.castSucc).val = i.val := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_castSucc i`."
  intro i
  exact Fin.val_castSucc i

Conclusion "
`Fin.castSucc` is an **embedding**: it maps `Fin n` into
`Fin (n+1)` without changing any values. Think of it as
*widening the frame* — the elements stay in place, but the
type now has room for one more element at the top.

**Why not automatic coercion?** You might wonder why Lean doesn't
just automatically treat `Fin n` elements as `Fin (n+1)` elements.
The reason: Lean's type system doesn't have a built-in coercion
from `Fin n` to `Fin m` for arbitrary `n <= m`. Each embedding must
be explicit, and `castSucc` is the standard one for the `n -> n+1`
step. This explicitness prevents silent type-widening bugs.

(Since the definition is transparent, `rfl` also works here —
just as it did for `Fin.val_last` in Level 1. But
`Fin.val_castSucc` is the tool you'll need when `n` is a
variable, so practice using the named lemma.)

The element that occupies that new top position is `Fin.last n`
(from Level 1). So `Fin (n+1)` consists of:
- The images of `Fin.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$)
- `Fin.last n` (value $n$)

You'll prove this decomposition in the boss level.

In plain math: the inclusion $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\} \\hookrightarrow
\\{0, \\ldots, n\\}$ is the identity on elements.
"

/-- `Fin.castSucc` embeds `Fin n` into `Fin (n + 1)` by keeping
the same value but relaxing the upper bound.

## Syntax
```
i.castSucc      -- dot notation
Fin.castSucc i  -- explicit
```

## Type
`Fin.castSucc : Fin n → Fin (n + 1)`

## Value
`i.castSucc.val = i.val` (see `Fin.val_castSucc`)

## When to use it
When you need to treat an element of `Fin n` as an element of
`Fin (n + 1)` without changing its value.
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.castSucc as "Fin.castSucc"

/-- `Fin.val_castSucc i` states that `i.castSucc.val = i.val`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_castSucc]      -- rewrites i.castSucc.val to i.val
exact Fin.val_castSucc i   -- closes the goal directly
```

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `castSucc` to the underlying value.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_castSucc as "Fin.val_castSucc" in "Fin"

NewDefinition Fin.castSucc
NewTheorem Fin.val_castSucc

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 4

Title "Moving Forward: Fin.succ"

Introduction "
# Fin.succ: The Next Element

There's a second way to go from `Fin n` to `Fin (n+1)`:
**`Fin.succ`**, which **increments** the value by 1.

If `i : Fin n` has value `k`, then `i.succ : Fin (n+1)` has
value `k + 1`.

Compare with `castSucc`:
- `i.castSucc.val = i.val` (same value)
- `i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (value plus one)

Both send `Fin n → Fin (n+1)`, but they land at different
positions. `castSucc` keeps you in place; `succ` moves you
forward by one.

The theorem **`Fin.val_succ`** states:
$$\\texttt{i.succ.val} = \\texttt{i.val} + 1$$

**Your task**: Prove this value relationship.
"

/-- `Fin.succ` increments the underlying value by 1. -/
Statement : ∀ i : Fin 3, (i.succ).val = i.val + 1 := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use `exact Fin.val_succ i`."
  intro i
  exact Fin.val_succ i

Conclusion "
(As with `Fin.val_last` and `Fin.val_castSucc`, `rfl` also works
here since the definition is transparent. But `Fin.val_succ` is
the rewrite tool you'll need when `n` is a variable.)

You now have the three navigation functions and their val lemmas:

| Function | Type | Value |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin.last n` | `Fin (n+1)` | `n` |
| `i.castSucc` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val` (unchanged) |
| `i.succ` | `Fin n → Fin (n+1)` | `i.val + 1` |

The val lemmas (`val_last`, `val_castSucc`, `val_succ`) convert
`Fin` navigation into plain arithmetic. The strategy for proving
`Fin` facts is: **rewrite to values, then omega**.

In the next level, you'll combine these val lemmas to prove a
relationship between `castSucc` and `succ`.
"

/-- `Fin.succ` maps `Fin n` to `Fin (n + 1)` by adding 1 to the
underlying value.

## Syntax
```
i.succ      -- dot notation
Fin.succ i  -- explicit
